coredata.dc:title: Rational design and construction of a mesoporous silica-supported ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of nicosulfuron
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haochi
Lei, Xiangmin
Zhu, Lin
Chen, Ligang
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: The excessive use of pesticides is an urgent issue facing environmental sustainability and human health. In this study, a uniform dispersion size, good fluorescence performance and mesoporous structure of a ratiometric fluorescent probe were constructed for nicosulfuron detection. A solvent-free in situ solid-phase synthesis method was used to encapsulate biomass carbon dots within mesoporous silica (CDs@mSiO₂), followed by the modification of l-cysteine-modified manganese-doped zinc sulfide quantum dots (ZnS:Mn QDs), to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of nicosulfuron. This design effectively prevents the aggregation of CDs and reduces interference between the two fluorescent signals. Nicosulfuron detection had a low detection limit of 0.082 μM. Density functional theory calculations were carried out to uncover the specific interactions between nicosulfuron and ZnS:Mn QDs. The process of fluorescence quenching is ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer. This work offers a special strategy to produce a ratiometric fluorescent probe and illustrate the mechanism, which is crucial for sensing and environmental engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Nicosulfuron
Density functional theory
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs
Fluorescent measurement and pretreatment of actual samples
Rational design and preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes
Study on the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of narcoculture in environmental samples and method comparison

This work was supported by the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. TJS202102) and Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. HCK202112).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of synthesis process of ratiometric fluorescent probe.

(A) TEM image of ratiometric fluorescent probe, (B) XRD pattern of CDs@mSiO2, l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn and ratiometric fluorescent probe, (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs@mSiO2, (D) FT-IR spectra of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn, (E) FT-IR spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probe, and (F) TG, DTG curves of ratiometric fluorescent probe.

(A) High-resolution XPS of C1s, Si2p and O1s of CDs@mSiO2 composite, (B) high-resolution XPS of C1s, Si2p, O1s, S2p, Zn2p, and Mn2p spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probe. (C) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, (D) pore size distribution of ratiometric fluorescent probe.

(A) Effect of equilibrium time on the fluorescence intensity of ratiometric fluorescent probe. (B) Effect of pH on the fluorescence signal of ratiometric fluorescent probe and the ratiometric fluorescent probe with the addition of nicosulfuron. (C) Fluorescence spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probe with nicosulfuron. (D) Effect of nicosulfuron change on I459/I599 (n = 3).

(A) The electronic excitation of ZnS:Mn QDs. Cyan colors represent decreased charge and green colors represent increased charge (B) Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism (C) ESP distribution map of nicosulfuron and ZnS:Mn QDs. The positive and negative potentials are shown in blue and red(D) Sign(λ2)ρ colored IGMH scatter plot and colored function δginter maps of the conformations between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron (0.005 a.u. isosurfaces) (E) UV spectra of nicosulfuron and fluorescence spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probe (F) Conformational map of the system at the beginning frame of AIMD simulation (G) Conformational map of the system at the final frame of AIMD simulation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) Effects of common ions (B) molecules and other pesticides on the fluorescence response of ratiometric fluorescent probe.
Detection of nicosulfuron in water samples.
Comparison with other methods for nicosulfuron detection.

row.entry: Stirring extraction with PBS-methanol followed by SPE with C18
Liquid phase microextraction with hollow fiber-protected magnetized solvent-bar
Ultrasonic extraction with methanol-phosphate buffer followed by SPE with C18

head.ce:title: Rational design and construction of a mesoporous silica-supported ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of nicosulfuron

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excessive use of pesticides is an urgent issue facing environmental sustainability and human health. In this study, a uniform dispersion size, good fluorescence performance and mesoporous structure of a ratiometric fluorescent probe were constructed for nicosulfuron detection. A solvent-free in situ solid-phase synthesis method was used to encapsulate biomass carbon dots within mesoporous silica (CDs@mSiO₂), followed by the modification of l-cysteine-modified manganese-doped zinc sulfide quantum dots (ZnS:Mn QDs), to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of nicosulfuron. This design effectively prevents the aggregation of CDs and reduces interference between the two fluorescent signals. Nicosulfuron detection had a low detection limit of 0.082 μM. Density functional theory calculations were carried out to uncover the specific interactions between nicosulfuron and ZnS:Mn QDs. The process of fluorescence quenching is ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer. This work offers a special strategy to produce a ratiometric fluorescent probe and illustrate the mechanism, which is crucial for sensing and environmental engineering.

fig. 1 The schematic illustration of synthesis process of ratiometric fluorescent probe.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nicosulfuron, as an efficient and low toxicity sulfonylurea herbicide, is extensively used to control the growth of cereals, broad-leaved weeds and some grasses [1]. However, plants exhibit widespread susceptibility to nicosulfuron, which has the characteristics of high solubility, high fluidity and slow degradation. If released into the environment, it endangers human health and may have potential adverse impacts on the ecosystem [2]. The limit of sulfonylurea herbicide in drinking water, surface water and industrial wastewater in the European Union are 0.1, 1 and 10 μg/L, respectively [3]. Therefore, it is a prominence challenge to establish a reliable and accurate analysis and detection algorithm for nicosulfuron in environmental samples.

Currently, nicosulfuron is mainly detected by capillary electrophoresis [4], LC-MS [5] and so forth. Liquid chromatography has high sensitivity, but the complex operation procedure limits its development. The capillary electrophoresis method has the problem of low sensitivity. Based on this, a strategy was proposed for nicosulfuron detection by fluorescence-based method. Carbon dots (CDs), as an indispensable portion of carbon material family, have good chemical stability and low toxicity. Among them, biomass materials that can serve as carbon precursors have drawn a lot of interest with diverse sources, inexpensive and large-scale synthesis [6,7]. Cellulose is the main polysaccharide in plants and is considered as environmentally friendly biomaterials [8]. It is a type of widely linear chain carbohydrate polymer and encompasses plenty of hydroxyl groups. This material exhibits high specific surface area, superior tensile strength and good biocompatibility [9], generating it an excellent precursor for biomass CDs production. To maintain stability and avoid secondary pollution, CDs are generally encapsulated in other materials in the detection process of complex systems, such as molecularly imprinted polymers [10], metal organic frameworks [11] and cyclodextrins [12]. The selectivity and sensitivity of the synthesized functional materials were improved. However, the manufacturing process was intricate and difficult to surface functionalization. A strategy was proposed to directly encapsulate biomass CDs in mesoporous silica through one-step pyrolysis of cellulose, which not merely simplifies experimental operation, but also avoids consuming large amounts of organic solvents. Mesoporous silica as an ideal encapsulation carrier offers the advantages of adjustable size, regular and continuous pores, high specific surface area and easy surface modification. Simultaneously, it is conducive to sensitive variations in the fluorescence signals and effective recognition of target molecules [13].

Previous studies have developed a large number of fluorescent probes for pesticide detection based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism or target-triggered fluorescence enhancement effect [14,15]. However, single-signal fluorescent probes may be affected by instrument efficiency, environmental conditions and other factors, resulting in weak anti-interference ability, poor selectivity and low sensitivity. Therefore, improving the anti-interference ability of methods in complex systems is another significant challenge. Incorporating an additional reference emission to construct a ratiometric fluorescenct probe could be a viable approach for solving the aforementioned problems. The ratiometric fluorescence can eliminate the impact of environmental factors, such as shifts in the light resource or detector and changes in the probe concentration, ultimately enhancing the accuracy of detection [16]. This kind of ratiometric fluorescent probe is dependent on analyte-induced changes in the fluorescence intensity and can provide a built-in environmental interference correction, effectively eliminating fluctuations in light excitation intensity [17]. Zhu et al. [18] combined CDs with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to prepare CDs-FITC ratiometric fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. Shuang et al. [19]. prepared a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Janus silica nanoparticles where FITC and rhodamine B were functionalized for intracellular pH detection. This kind of probe preparation process is simple and feasible, but the ratiometric fluorescent probe made of organic dyes generally has drawbacks such as susceptibility to photobleaching, emission bandwidth and narrow excitation range. Some researchers constructed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for Ag+ [20] and salicylic acid [21] detection. However, in the process of constructing ratiometric fluorescent probes, little attention has been paid to design experimental from a theoretical calculation perspective. Thus, it is crucial to prepare ratiometric fluorescent probes based on biomass CDs in a simple, low-cost and efficient manner with a combination of theoretical calculations and experiments for rapid pesticides detection.

Herein, a solvent-free, facile high-temperature solid-state pyrolysis method was designed, using nanocellulose and tetramethoxysilane as raw materials, to synthesize CDs@mSiO2 through a one-step method. The porosity and large surface area of CDs@mSiO2 not only has good dispersibility and easy modification, but also can pre-enrich target molecules. CDs@mSiO2 also makes it an excellent carrier, which not only effectively avoids the fluorescence quenching effect induced by CDs self-aggregation but also enhances the mass transfer rate, thus improving the efficiency of target detection. Additionally, the designed CDs@mSiO2 nanoparticles were further combined with l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe that can effectively detect nicosulfuron in water. l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs can function as a site for nicosulfuron interaction. The principal mechanisms of the fluorescence quenching are identified as the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from the probe to nicosulfuron. Clearly, to improve the sensitivity, a combination of the interaction between the ratiometric fluorescent probe and target analytes, as well as the structure-spatial match of them was considered. The density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependence DFT (TD-DFT) calculations indicated the structure of probe and its fluorescence quenching mechanism after adding nicosulfuron in the system. Furthermore, the AIMD simulation was used to elucidate their interactions of the binding and departure process at the atomic level. In practical applications, the ratiometric fluorescent probe, with its unique functionalities, has the potential to serve as an excellent tool for nicosulfuron detection.
The reagents and instrument were provided in the Supporting Information Materials.

Nanocellulose was synthesized by pyrolysis method. 20 g of cellulose powder was added to 300 mL of 64 % H2SO4 with vigorously stirring for 1 h at 50 °C for hydrolysis reaction. After completing the reaction, the system was rapidly diluted with 3000 mL of cold water and washed three times by centrifugation at 7500 rpm. The white suspension obtained by centrifugation was dialysis (MWCO: 12000–14000) until the solution was neutral. The dialyzed solution was dispersed with ultrasound for 10 min, and a transparent, stable and uniformly dispersed nanocellulose suspension was obtained by centrifugation.

TMOS (0.60 mL, 2.7 mmol) was mixed with nanocellulose suspension (10 mL), stirred for 1 h and dried for further use. The prepared solid product was put into a muffle furnace, heated at a rate of 120 °C/h for 2 h and reached 100 °C. Subsequently, the temperature was further increased to 540 °C maintaining the same heating rate for 6 h, so that the cellulose was carbonized at high temperature to generate CDs accompanied by mesoporous silica formation. After cooling to room temperature, CDs wrapped in mesoporous silica (CDs@mSiO2) composites were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs
ce:section.ce:para: MnCl2·4H2O (2 mmol) and ZnSO4·7H2O (25 mmol) were mixed into high-purity water (80 mL) at room temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, Na2S·9H2O (10 mL, 25 mmol) was introduced with stirring for 30 min to prepare ZnS:Mn QDs. Following this, l-cysteine (10 mL, 1.25 mmol) was incorporated into the system with stirring for 20 min. Finally, the product was centrifuged three times with ethanol to obtain l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs, and dried in a drying oven for further use.

To synthesize the ratiometric fluorescent probe, 42 mg of DCC, 20 mg of DMAP, 200 mg of CDs@mSiO2, and 15 mg l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs were added to 10 mL of dichloromethane solution and continued reacting for 24 h at room temperature. The ratiometric fluorescent probe was obtained by centrifugation at 7800 r/min, and washed three times with water and ethanol, respectively. Similarly, a non-ratiometric fluorescent probe without CDs@mSiO2 was well prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent measurement and pretreatment of actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: For nicosulfuron detection, a certain amount of different nicosulfuron standard solutions was added to Tris-HCl buffer (100 μL, pH = 9) and fluorescent probe solution (200 μL), then finally diluted to 2 mL. The final concentration of the ratiometric fluorescent probe was 2.5 mg mL−1. After mixing thoroughly for 4 min, the ratiometric fluorescence signals were recorded at 315 nm excitation wavelength. Tap water, lake and river samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The actual samples were spiked with varying concentrations of nicosulfuron, and the nicosulfuron concentration was calculated using the corresponding calibration equation.

The initial ZnS crystal structure was acquired from the Materials Project download and then the surface (1 1 1) was cleaved. In the Mn-doped ZnS model, four surface Zn per unit cell was replaced with Mn. To obtain more accurate results, geometric optimization using density functional theory (DFT) was performed for ZnS (1 1 1) and MnZnS (1 1 1). CP2K was used to carry out all DFT calculations [22]. The ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron geometries were investigated with exchange-correlation Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional with Grimme third-generation dispersion, and the DZVP-MOLOPT-SR-GTH basis set, plane wave cutoff was set to 450 eV.

The vacuum layer of 12 Å was set in the z direction. Energy convergence tests were performed such that the total energy difference was less than 10−6 eV. Vibration analysis was employed to determine the true minima surface of refined structure on the potential energy with no false frequencies present. The optimized geometries of nicosulfuron and ZnS:Mn QDs were utilized to investigate the favorable conformation by studying the adsorption energy. The adsorption energy (Eads) including ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron was computed as follows:
(1)
E ads
=
E
(
QDs + analyte
)
−
(
E QDs
+
E analyte
)
Where EQDs + analyte means the total energy of ZnS:Mn QDs-nicosulfuron, EQDs is the ZnS:Mn QDs energy, while Eanalyte is nicosulfuron energy. Independent Gradient Model based on the Hirshfeld partition (IGMH) [23] and Electrostatic potential (ESP) [24] were used to examine the adsorption mechanism between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron. Multiwfn was used to perform ESP and IGMH calculations [25]. The molden file generated by CP2K was utilized, and the [Cell] field was added into the molden file. The molden file was employed as an input file for IGMH, and the pertinent calculations were conducted in multiwfn, where nicosulfuron and ZnS:Mn QDs were defined as two fragments, for the purpose of examining mechanism. VMD software was applied to investigate weak interactions and ESP. All calculations pertaining to the excited state were performed using the TD-DFT method to assess the DFT to ascertain the optical excitation. In TD-DFT calculations, 50 excited states were calculated. To reduce the associated computational costs, the Tamm-Dancoff approximation was employed in our calculations.

To advance the theoretical understanding of this subject, ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations were used to elucidate the dynamic mechanism. Some parameters were provided in the Supporting Information Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational design and preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: This work proposed a one-step rapid solid-state reaction to directly synthesize blue-emitting CDs@mSiO2 powder. This reaction needs no solvent and the product is employed as a reference fluorescent label for nicosulfuron detection. The possible mechanisms for preparing biomass CDs using cellulose as a carbon source were as follows [26]: Cellulose is decomposed into monosaccharides and polysaccharides under high-temperature pyrolysis conditions. Polysaccharides are further decomposed into monomers. Simultaneously, fructose is produced by isomerization, while 1,2,4-benzenetriol, hydroxymethylfurfural derivatives and aldehydes are formed through dehydration and cracking reactions. Subsequently, these intermediate products undergo condensation or polymerization reactions triggered by aldol condensation or intermolecular dehydration, ultimately were generating biomass CDs. These structures of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and furfural are interconnected through processes such as polymerization and condensation, and their side chain ends contain groups like alkanes, carboxyl groups and so on. CDs exhibited a long-term stable fluorescence due to their special structure. On top of that, we further prepared a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on CDs@mSiO2 and l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs.

Fig. 1 shows the synthesis process of a ratiometric fluorescent probe. The biomass CDs are synthesized from cellulose, which is rich in hydroxyl groups. TMOS is added in the process of preparing biomass CDs, and the solid-state synthesis of CDs@mSiO2 composite material is successfully achieved through a one-step process. This material has high porosity and large surface area, which enabled them to absorb and pre-enrich target molecules, thereby leading to enhance sensitivity performance. Furthermore, l-cysteine was used as an amino acid for the synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent probes to alter ZnS:Mn QDs surface. The carboxyl groups of this ligand offer good water solubility, while the sulfur atom of the thiol group allows it to bind to the ZnS:Mn QDs surface. Afterwards, ZnS:Mn QDs have been modified with carboxyl groups that can link chemically to the hydroxyl-rich CDs@mSiO2 through a condensation reaction and catalyzed with DCC/DMAP. The prepared sensor demonstrates two distinct fluorescence emission peaks upon excitation of 315 nm. Biomass CDs are employed as reference signals in ratiometric fluorescent probes on account of chemical inertness and strong photostability. The characteristic peak of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs emitting red light at 599 nm belongs to typical transition between the 4T1 and 6A1 energy levels of Mn2+ 3d states [27–29]. It is worth noting that the red emission of ZnS:Mn QDs at 599 nm is quenched by nicosulfuron, while the blue fluorescence at 459 nm remains largely unaffected. The quenching of red light at 599 nm indicates an electron induced transfer process between l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron.

The effects of different pyrolysis time and Mn2+ doping on the fluorescence intensity were discussed in the Supporting Information Materials. As shown in Fig. S1, the pyrolysis time of preparing CDs@mSiO2 composites was set to 6 h. The molar ratio of ZnSO4 to MnCl2 was adjusted to 50:4 for further use.

The optical features of ratiometric fluorescent probe were also investigated using multiple characterization methods. Fig. 2 A shows that the average diameter of the synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probe is around 200 nm with uniform dispersion size. The morphology of CDs@mSiO2 composites was also provided in Fig. S2. Fig. 2B shows the XRD spectra of CDs@mSiO2, l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs and the synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probes. The broad peak of CDs@mSiO2 is located at 2θ = 22°, indicating an amorphous carbon structure. The characteristic peaks of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs are attributed to the diffraction peaks (111), (220) and (311), generatgenerating by the cubic crystal phase. In addition, the XRD peaks of the ratiometric fluorescent probe include the characteristic peak of carbon and the typical peaks of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs, further illustrating the successful preparation.

The structural information of CDs@mSiO2, l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs and ratiometric fluorescent probes was revealed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2C shows that CDs@mSiO2 contains Si–O–Si group (1083 cm−1), Si–O bond (806 cm−1) and CO group (1618 cm−1). The characteristic peaks at 1354 cm−1 and 3457 cm−1 represent the presence of C–O and –OH groups, respectively. For l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs, the typical peaks of C–O and CO groups are seen at 1390 cm−1 and 1603 cm−1(Fig. 2D). The peaks at 3427 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 suggested the existence of N–H or –OH group and C–H group. While the characteristic absorption peak of thiol group (-SH) disappears at 2550-2700 cm−1, because of the simple covalent bond between thiol group and the surface of ZnS. Therefore, it is proved that l-cysteine has been effectively conjugated to the ZnS:Mn QDs surface.

In the FT-IR spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probes, the peaks corresponding to Si–O and Si–O–Si vibrations are investigated at 1087 cm−1 and 804 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2E). The broad peak at 3431 cm−1 becomes smaller, indicating the successful binding between CDs@mSiO2 and l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs. The CO group's typical peak also shifts red to 1650 cm−1. CDs@mSiO2 and l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs, which were physically mixed in proportion, were used as contrasts. The characteristic peaks of the prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe are essentially consistent with those of a simple mixture of two quantum dots, indicating the successful fabrication.

Thermogravimetric analysis was utilized to characterize the thermal stability. Fig. 2F displays DTG and TG curves of the ratiometric fluorescent probe under N2 conditions within 45–800 °C temperature range. The final weight loss of the ratiometric fluorescent probe is 5.1 %. The result indicates that the ratiometric fluorescent probe has good thermal stability and can meet the requirements of practical applications.

To further confirm the elemental composition, XPS characterization is used to identify the functional groups present in both the CDs@mSiO2 and ratiometric fluorescent probe, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there are mainly three elements of carbon, silicon and oxygen in CDs@mSiO2 in addition to hydrogen. The C1s spectra in CDs@mSiO2 contain four characteristic binding energy peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.2 eV and 286.9 eV, owing to the existence of sp2C, C–C, C–O and CO groups. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Si2p mainly has two fitting peaks at 103.3 eV and 104.2 eV, indicating that there are two type structures of Si–OH and Si–O–Si in CDs@mSiO2. The fitted peaks of O1s at 532.7 eV, 533.5 eV and 534.3 eV belong to Si–O, CO and C–O–H groups, respectively.

After combining CDs@mSiO2 with l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs to prepare a ratiometric fluorescent probe, the corresponding binding mode of different functional groups is evident from the XPS spectra (Fig. 3B). In the C1s XPS spectrum, the primary characteristic peaks of sp2C, C–C, C–O, and CO are still present in the synthesized composite material. The characteristic peaks of O1s high-resolution energy spectrum indicate that Si–O (532.7 eV), CO (533.4 eV) and C–O–H (534.1 eV) are still the main forms of oxygen. The spectrum of Si2p also confirms the presence of Si–OH (102.9 eV) and Si–O–Si (103.8 eV). Additionally, the characteristic peaks of l-cysteine modified ZnS:Mn QDs in the ratiometric fluorescent probe still exist. The S2p spectrum mainly has two fitting peaks located at 161.7 eV and 162.7 eV, which was due to the presence of S2p3/2 and S2p1/2. The peaks at 1024.7 eV and 1047.9 eV confirm the existence of Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2. The XPS spectrum showed a weak intensity of Mn2+, while the red light emission at 599 nm is clearly visible in the sensor's fluorescence spectrum. Since the red emission of ZnS:Mn QDs at 599 nm belonged to the 4T1-6A1 transition of Mn2+ [30], it indicated that Mn2+ was successfully doped into ZnS QDs, which can be adopted as a substitute for Zn atoms or interstitial atoms [31]. The effective doping of Mn element is further demonstrated by the XRD spectrum. As the doping amount of Mn2+ increases within a certain range, there is a notable increase in the emission peak's intensity, which subsequently declines as the doping amount continues to increase. The high concentration of Mn2+ doping can be used to explain this phenomenon by its quenching effect, which is further proving that Mn2+ doping is successful. From XPS and FT-IR spectra, the surface of CDs@mSiO2 material has multiple oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc. The functional groups endowed the material with good hydrophilicity and thus enabled a stable dispersion in water.

The mesoporous structure of CDs@mSiO2 and ratiometric fluorescent probe can be verified by N2 adsorption experiment. Based on the IUPAC categorization, a type IV isothermal adsorption line can be seen in Fig. 3C and D and Fig.S3, which belongs to the typical mesoporous material adsorption and exhibits monolayer saturated adsorption characteristics, with capillary condensation occurring at relative pressure of 0.4–0.8. The specific surface area of ratiometric fluorescence probe calculated by the BET algorithm was 515 m2 g−1, which is slightly lower than that of CDs@mSiO2 (532 m2 g−1). The average mesoporous pore size of the ratiometric fluorescent probe was calculated to be 2.2 nm by BJH pore size distribution function. The results showed that the material had a mesoporous structure, which can reduce mass transfer resistance and provide high binding ability and rapid adsorption/desorption ability, making the synthesized material more appropriate for fluorescent probes construction.

The effect of different excitation wavelengths (280–350 nm) was also investigated in the Supporting Information Materials. As depicted in Fig. S4, the optimal excitation wavelength of ratiometric fluorescent probe was set at 315 nm.

Equilibrium time. The impact of different binding times was also investigated, as depicted in Fig. 4 A. The findings demonstrated that the addition of nicosulfuron led to a gradual decline in the fluorescence intensity at 599 nm, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 459 nm remained basically unchanged, causing the fluorescence intensity ratio (I459/I599) to gradually increase and reach a stable value after 4 min. Therefore, 4 min were set as the equilibrium time for the reaction system.

The effect of pH. The pH level is identified as a significant determinant affecting the system's fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I459/I599) first decreased (pH = 3–6), then remained stable (pH = 7–10), and then increased (pH = 11–12) without adding nicosulfuron in Fig. 4B. The reason for this phenomenon is that the weakening of the thiol bond in the stabilizer modified on ZnS:Mn QDs surface, it dissociates from QDs [32], which leads to a lower fluorescence intensity of red-emitting QDs, so that I459/I599 ratio is higher under acidic conditions. With increasing pH, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@mSiO2 kept unchanged, while the carboxyl groups on ZnS:Mn QDs surface become negatively charged, which is conducive to its better dispersion and stronger fluorescence. At this time, the ratio of I459/I599 decreased. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I459/I599 remained stable while the corresponding quantum dots kept stable (pH = 7–10). When the pH is under strong alkaline conditions, the fluorescence peak at 459 nm is relatively stable. In contrast, the formation of metal hydroxides on the surface of l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs leads to a decrease in their fluorescence intensity [33], which in turn causes an increase in the I459/I599 ratio.

The ratiometric fluorescent probe showed the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency at pH = 9 after involvement of nicosulfuron. As pH increases or decreases, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreases. On this basis, we attribute the reason for this phenomenon to pKa = 4.6 of nicosulfuron. In the process of protonation/deprotonation, nicosulfuron exhibits different ionic states under different pH conditions. When pH < 4.6, nicosulfuron is positively charged, and exhibits electrostatic repulsion with l-cysteine (pK a1 = 2.35, pK a2 = 9.05) in the positively charged ratiometric fluorescent probe, resulting in a lower degree of fluorescence quenching. As 4.6 < pH < 9, nicosulfuron is negatively charged, while the ratiometric fluorescent probe is uncharged, leading to an improved fluorescence quenching efficiency. When pH > 9, the ratiometric fluorescent probe and nicosulfuron were negatively charged, resulting in mutual repulsion and a reduction in fluorescence quenching efficiency. Therefore, the experimental conditions were set to pH = 9.

Calibration curve and detection limit. Under optimal conditions, the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescent probe was evaluated by adding different nicosulfuron concentrations and measuring the fluorescence response. The fluorescence intensity of red-emitting QDs decreases continuously, whereas the CDs@mSiO2 fluorescence intensity remains almost unchanged with an increase in nicosulfuron concentration (Fig. 4C). The I459/I599 ratio was linearly related to the nicosulfuron concentration (0.25–5.0 μM) as illustrated in Fig. 4D. The linear equation is as follows: I459/I599 = 0.1872 × Cnicosulfuron+0.8723, R2 = 0.993. The LOD of nicosulfuron was 0.082 μM with LOD = 3σ/k. In this formula, σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank parallel measurement signals, k represents the linear equation slope. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I459/I599) of ratiometric fluorescent probe and non-ratiometric signals (I599) of ZnS:Mn QDs were also compared in Fig. S5. The RSDs of ratiometric and non-ratiometric fluorescent probe were calculated as 1.9 % and 9.3 %, respectively. Considering this, the ratiometric fluorescent probe has desirable stability than non-ratiometric probe, thereby avoiding unnecessary interference and bringing more reliable data.

Mn2+ has an electronic layer structure of 1s22s22p63s23p63d5. The emission peak of manganese in ZnS:Mn QDs is triggered by d-d transition. In essence, the luminescence process of ZnS:Mn QDs is as follows (1–3):
(1a)
Mn
2 +
+
h +
( VB )
→ Μ
n
3 +
(2)
Mn
3 +
+ e →
(
Mn
2 +
)
∗
(3)
(
Mn
2 +
)
∗ →
Mn
2 +
+ h
v 2
As quantum dots are excited by light, electron-hole pairs are separated, in which the electron-hole pair is captured by Mn2+, and then Mn3+ combines with an electron to form the excited state of Mn2+. When Mn2+ returns to the ground state, fluorescence is emitted. The electronic transition law elucidates that the transition on the d5 electron layer of Mn2+ are prohibited. However, the position of Zn2+ is replaced by Mn2+ doping into the lattice of ZnS crystal. As a result of the surrounding crystal field, the low energy level provided by the main quantum dot lattice is integrated into Mn2+ wide band gap, so that the electronic structure changes and part of the transition band gap is lifted. Simultaneously, the energy level transition of electrons in Mn2+ between 4T1 and 6A1 is achieved, forming ZnS:Mn QDs. Compared with the defect-related emission of ZnS QDs, ion doping makes the fluorescence emission band of quantum dots more stable and controllable, because the defect peaks of semiconductor nanoparticles are conveniently affected by synthesis process or environmental factors.

Pesticide-related luminescence sensing techniques can primarily rely on the following mechanisms such as fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET), PET and so forth [34]. In this work, the fluorescence was selectively quenched at 599 nm after adding nicosulfuron to the system. TDDFT was employed to investigate the optical gap of ZnS:Mn QDs, with the objective of deepening the understanding of nicosulfuron sensing mechanism [35]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the distribution of electrons shifts significantly after excitation, indicating that ZnS:Mn QDs exhibit characteristics of charge-transfer excitation. Both sulfur and nitrogen atoms of nicosulfuron may interact with ZnS:Mn QDs. This interaction generates electron transfer between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron, and the energy level of nicosulfuron can correspond to the 4T1 energy level of Mn2+ 3d state (Fig. 5B). The excitation energy of nicosulfuron is 4.50 eV according to theoretical calculations, which does not produce excitation at the fluorescence wavelength under the experimental conditions, and does not affect the experimental results. The energy required to produce ZnS:Mn QDs excitation is increased by nicosulfuron influence. The excitation energy of ZnS:Mn QDs after binding nicosulfuron increases by 0.17 eV, in comparison to ZnS:Mn QDs. As a result, the luminescence emission of ZnS:Mn QDs was weakened, leading to fluorescence quenching.

Furthermore, the detection mechanism of target analyte was examined through the utilization of ESP distribution map. The region colored red signifies a positive electrostatic potential, which suggests an electrophilic nature and making nucleophilic attacks more likely to occur. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the localized surface electrostatic potential generated by the lone pair of electrons in the nitrogen atom is negative. The carbon atom bonded to the nitrogen atom carries a specific positive charge, which results in a positive electrostatic potential on the heterocyclic ring. The heterocyclic ring on the left side is comprised of one fewer number of nitrogen atoms than the ring on the right side. This results in a more pronounced red coloration of the electrostatic potential on the left heterocyclic ring than on the right side. It can be noticed that the minima of negative electrostatic potential in nicosulfuron will be in close proximity to the positively charged region of ZnS:Mn QDs, indicating the existence of intermolecular electrostatic attraction between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron. The most negative observed ESP value of nicosulfuron was −53.90 kcal mol−1.

Apart from the electrostatic interaction, IGMH assessment was applied to examine the strengths and types of weak interactions between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron. We optimized the geometry of ZnS:Mn QDs-nicosulfuron system and then plotted the IGMH diagram by defining ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron as two fragments, as depicted in Fig. 5D. The scatter plots of IGMH and the functional δginter map were colored from blue to red in the range of −0.025-0.025. Here, blue signifies strong interactions including hydrogen bonds, red denotes spatial repulsion and green indicates weak interactions such as van der Waals force. To investigate the connection with QDs and nicosulfuron, the δginter map simulated the conformations of QDs and nicosulfuron, and the optimized conformations of absorption energy were obtained (Eads = -11.033 kcal mol−1) through theoretical computations.

For enhanced visual representation, the correlation between peak and maximum values of δginter in the isosurface plot is illustrated by arrows. Additionally, the figure illustrates that regions exhibite greater interaction, indicated by higher values of ρ, tend to display larger δginter maxima within these regions. The region of maximum interaction between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron corresponds to a peak of Sign(λ2)ρ of −0.015. The region with the next largest interaction between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron corresponds to a peak of Sign(λ2)ρ of −0.008. As can be observed, the isosurfaces unambiguously demonstrate the region where the interaction between ZnS:Mn QDs and nicosulfuron is most pronounced. The prevalence of green on the isosurfaces indicates that the adsorption of nicosulfuron by ZnS:Mn QDs occurs predominantly through weak dispersion forces.

Subsequently, the nicosulfuron's UV absorption peak and the fluorescence spectrum were also measured to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. 5E). The UV absorption peak of nicosulfuron is at 238 nm, and the characteristic fluorescence peaks of ratiometric fluorescent probe are at 459 nm and 599 nm, indicating no significant overlap. Thus, the possibility of FRET between nicosulfuron and the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be ruled out. To summarize, the PET effect is essential to fluorescence quenching mechanism for detecting nicosulfuron. Fig. 5F and G showed the system conformational at the beginning and final frame of AIMD simulation. To advance the theoretical understanding of this subject, AIMD simulations were shown in Fig. S6, AIMD trajectories indicated that the right group (-N, N-dimethylnicotinamide) of nicosulfuron is moving away from ZnS:Mn QDs, while the sulfamoyl group is gradually moving closer to the ZnS:Mn QDs as the simulation progresses. The AIMD trajectories corroborated the accuracy of ESP and IGMH results in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: For assessing the selectivity, the effects of various ions, small biomolecules and other pesticides were studied under the same conditions (Fig. 6 ). There is no significant change in I459/I599 ratio, when other ions (Na+、K+、Ca2+、Mg2+、Al3+、SO4 2−、NO3 −、Cl−) are present under the same concentration conditions. EDTA was employed as a masking agent to eliminate the interference of Cu2+ to enhance selectivity and precision, considering that the probe's fluorescence is slightly quenched by Cu2+. The same concentrations of histidine, valine, lysine, aspartic acid, methionine, tryptophan, fructose, glucose and sucrose were also added to the fluorescence system. These substances have little effect on the detection system. Additionally, the same concentrations of metaflumizone, spirodiclofen and avermectin were added as interferents. In the meantime, the adsorption energies of ratiometric fluorescent probes for other pesticides were theoretically calculated. According to the experimental results, metaflumizone, spirodiclofen and avermectin have little effect on the detection system, verifying that the ratiometric fluorescent probe has good selectivity for nicosulfuron detection. The relevant results were shown in Fig. 6B. Based on the theoretical calculation, compared with other pesticides (metaflumizone, spirodiclofen and avermectin), the adsorption energy of nicosulfuron was the lowest, which has strongest ability to bind with the ratiometric fluorescent probe, the simulation results agreed well with the experimental results(Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of narcoculture in environmental samples and method comparison
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe was evaluated by analyzing river water, lake water and tap water samples to examine whether it satisfied actual demand. Nicosulfuron was not detected in water samples within the detection range. Therefore, different concentrations of nicosulfuron (0.25 μM, 2.50 μM and 5 μM) were added to the water samples as a corresponding spike recovery factor experiment. In the detection of spiked water samples (Table 1 ), the standard deviations of nicosulfuron are 1.0%–4.5%, and the recovery factors are 94.0%–101.8%, indicating that the method has high accuracy and good applicability in the analysis of actual water samples.

Table 2 compares the proposed method with other reported methods for nicosulfuron detection. At present, the main methods for detecting nicosulfuron are liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, etc. The merit of liquid chromatography lies in lower LOD, but the detection time is longer, while the utility of fluorescence detection methods can greatly shorten the detection time and improve efficiency. Compared with capillary electrophoresis, the constructed detection method can effectively improve the sensitivity and has a relatively low detection limit. The synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probe features simple fabrication, rapid detection and good sensitivity.

In this work, CDs@mSiO2 and l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs are adopted as optical elements, with blue-emitting CDs@mSiO2 as the reference signal and red-emitting l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs as the response signal. Subsequently, a ratiometric fluorescent probe with stable performance was synthesized by covalent bonding between CDs@mSiO2 and l-cysteine-modified ZnS:Mn QDs. The material has uniform dispersion size, good fluorescence performance and mesoporous structure, which was proved by a series of characterization methods. The presence of mesoporous silica can effectively improve the stability of fluorescent signals and further enrich the target analytes. After adding nicosulfuron to the system, ZnS:Mn QDs fluorescence was effectively quenched, while CDs@mSiO2 fluorescence remained relatively stable and unchanged. In addition, the synthesis process and quenching mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescent probe were also discussed through computational simulation assistance, proving the importance of the PET effect. Finally, the material was applied to detect nicosulfuron in actual environmental water samples, and the RSDs and recoveries were 1.0%–4.5% and 94.0%–101.8%, respectively. The constructed method can greatly reduce the interference of other factors, thereby enabling rapid and accurate detection of nicosulfuron.

Haochi Liu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiangmin Lei: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Lin Zhu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Ligang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software. Lan Ding: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, China (No. TJS202102) and Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, China (No. HCK202112).

Acta 186 (2019) 486-496.
